range.conservationStatus: Common in suitable habitat; not globally threatened
range.geographicRange: Eastern North America — associated with moist deciduous and mixed forests

elevationRange.note: Often in ravines and low moist woods in summer and early fall

ecology.role: Mycorrhizal partner with hardwood trees — exchanges nutrients with oak, beech, and birch roots while producing distinctive funnel-shaped fruiting bodies that release spores from the outer hymenium.

associatedSpecies.relationship: Often found in sugar maple–beech forest floors

habitat.summary: Moist, shaded forest floors — especially ravines, stream margins, and mature hardwood stands with deep leaf litter.
preferred: Sugar maple and beech forest floors
habitat.substrate: Damp leaf litter and humus over well-drained soil

phenology.seasonality: Summer through early fall fruiting — later than spring morels
phenology.activePeriod: July through September in northeastern Pennsylvania; peaks after sustained summer rain

regionalNotes.pike-county-pa: Check shaded ravines after two days of July rain — often grows in groups that are easy to walk past if you look at the trail instead of the forest floor.

identification.summary: A thin, vase- or trumpet-shaped black to gray-brown mushroom with a smooth outer surface and no true gills — the spore-bearing surface is on the outside of the funnel.
fieldMarks: Funnel or trumpet shape — hollow center
Dark gray to black (sometimes brownish when dry)
No true gills — underside smooth or slightly wrinkled
Fragile, thin flesh; often grows in clusters or arcs

lookAlikes.name: Golden chanterelle (Cantharellus spp.)
lookAlikes.distinction: Yellow-orange, thicker flesh, false gills (ridges), not funnel-shaped

lookAlikes.name: Ash-tree bolete (dark capped boletes)
lookAlikes.distinction: Has cap and stem with pores underneath — not a hollow trumpet

considerations: Generally considered edible when cooked — still require positive ID
Dry specimens may be harder to distinguish from dark cup fungi — verify fresh material

observation: Look down and sideways in ravines — trumpets are easy to miss
Photograph clusters in situ to learn growth patterns
Harvest sparingly if at all — cut or twist gently, leave young trumpets
leaveNoTrace: Ravine slopes erode easily — stay on established paths
Do not trample moss mats searching for fungi
ethics.conservation: Black trumpets are a reward for patient, ground-level observation — not a species to strip from a ravine patch.

tips: Low angle against moss or leaf litter shows trumpet shape
Include a hand or lens cap for scale — they are smaller than they appear in memory
Diffuse forest shade works better than direct sun on black caps

education.overview: The black trumpet rewards forest walkers who slow down in moist ravines — a lesson in looking at the forest floor as carefully as the canopy.
education.summary: Not a chanterelle, not a morel — a summer mycorrhizal trumpet that teaches ravine ecology.
interestingFacts: Spores develop on the outer surface — the opposite of gilled mushrooms
Often fruits in the same ravine zones year after year if habitat remains intact
Dries well for culinary use — but Waypoint teaches observation before harvest
fieldNotes: Walk ravines after rain with eyes on the ground 3–5 meters ahead
Note dominant trees — maple and beech presence is a useful context clue
Compare fruiting here to golden chanterelle timing later in summer

relatedSpecies.relationship: Spring predecessor on different substrate signal

futureObservations: Record date after rain event and dominant canopy species
Note whether trumpets grow in arcs — possible fairy ring pattern
Compare abundance in hemlock vs. pure hardwood ravines
unknowns: Exact host tree preferences vary by site — associations are patterns, not rules
Craterellus species boundaries are actively studied in North America

media.alt: Cluster of black trumpets in mossy ravine leaf litter
media.caption: Funnel-shaped fruiting bodies on a shaded forest floor — placeholder

research.notes: Candidate for future ForageCast summer model overlay
